lines: PRACTICAL PROJECT · MIINA HÄRMA GYMNASIUM · TARTU 2025
A  high-school  practical  project  in  which  two  students  designed,  built,  and  documented  a  fully
functional spark-gap Tesla coil from scratch — achieving 4 cm arc discharges and wireless LED
illumination using only a standard 220 V household socket.
This  project  grew  out  of  a  sustained  interest  in  electrophysics  developed  throughout  secondary  school.
Inspired by YouTube educator Styropyro — known for high-voltage demonstrations that combine rigorous
physics  with  accessible  presentation  —  the  two  authors  decided  to  build  a  working  Tesla  coil  as  their
The  decision  to  collaborate  was  deliberate:  shared  costs,  complementary  skills,  and  the  opportunity  to
approach problems from multiple angles. A budget ceiling of €200 was agreed at the outset, though the
final  build  ultimately  exceeded  this  by  €82  due  to  component  upgrades  made  during  the  iterative

A Tesla coil — invented by Nikola Tesla in 1891 — is a resonant transformer that converts mains electricity
into very high-frequency, very high-voltage alternating current. Tesla originally envisioned arrays of such
devices providing wireless power distribution across continents, but the technology proved too inefficient
for bulk energy transfer. Today Tesla coils are used almost exclusively for scientific demonstration.
Mains AC is stepped up by a high-voltage transformer and used to charge a capacitor bank. Once the capacitor
voltage exceeds the breakdown threshold of the spark gap, it discharges in a high-frequency burst through the
primary coil (L1). This oscillating current creates a rapidly changing magnetic field that induces a far higher voltage
in the secondary coil (L2), which has many more turns. The resulting electric field at the toroid terminal is strong
enough to ionise surrounding air, producing visible arc discharges.
An important safety property of Tesla coils is their high impedance: although the output voltage is extreme
(tens  of  thousands  of  volts),  the  current  that  can  flow  through  a  human  body  is  very  small,  and  the
frequency is high enough that it does not significantly stimulate nerve tissue. Direct contact with the output
is therefore survivable — provided the person is not grounded and carries no electronic medical devices.
Steps 220 V mains up to ~4 kV to charge the capacitor bank. Sourced from a salvaged microwave oven.
Stores charge and releases it in high-frequency bursts through the spark gap. Three capacitors in parallel, €64 total.
Air-gap switch: when voltage exceeds ~4 kV, it fires — discharging the capacitors through the primary coil at high frequency.
Low-turn flat spiral coil. Creates the oscillating magnetic field that drives the secondary. ~€50 in copper wire.
Tightly wound fine copper wire on a PVC tube. Many more turns than L1 — responsible for the voltage step-up.
Toroidal terminal at the top of L2. Concentrates the electric field and improves arc quality.
The transformer is submerged in mineral oil inside a sealed container to prevent internal short circuits.
Custom-built box with an emergency stop button, circuit breakers, and output connectors for safe operation.
Component procurement began on schedule in mid-2024. Certain parts — particularly the capacitors —
arrived only in early August, compressing the build window. The main construction sessions took place at
Roomet's family property near Otepää, which offered the necessary tools, workspace, and safe distance

25-page written report submitted to Miina Härma Gymnasium
The most significant iterative improvement was the transition from soldered connections to copper crimp
connectors  throughout  the  circuit.  This  dramatically  reduced  contact  resistance  and  improved  arc
performance. The transformer was also relocated into a sealed enclosure filled with mineral oil to eliminate
The  two-storey  structural  frame  —  fabricated  from  MDF  panels  —  separates  high-voltage  components
(lower  floor:  capacitors,  spark  gap,  oil-cooled  transformer)  from  the  resonator  assembly  (upper  floor:
primary coil, secondary coil, toroid). A grounding conductor runs through both floors and connects to the

The project was evaluated against four weighted criteria defined at the planning stage. Each criterion was
scored  on  a  1–5  scale,  with  descriptions  calibrated  to  the  specific  technical  and  safety  requirements  of
Arc discharges of 4 cm or longer are visible.
~4 cm arcs produced when a grounded metal object is brought near the toroid terminal.
Device can be operated safely after reading the manual; no specialist electrical knowledge required.
Clear bilingual manual supplied. No specialist training needed, but manual must be read before use.
Easy to operate; requires only a standard 220 V socket.
Two standard 220 V plugs required; no external ground connection; no industrial supply needed.
Painted/finished surfaces; cables neatly routed with minor exposed sections.
Frame painted; cables organised; lower floor partially open for cooling and access.
The overall experience was highly positive.

The  project  successfully  delivered  a  functional  spark-gap  Tesla  coil  that  meets  all  core  performance
benchmarks:  visible  arc  discharges,  wireless  illumination  via  induction,  safe  operation  from  a  standard
household socket, and a documented safety protocol. Both authors assign the project an overall grade of 4
The primary areas for future improvement are arc power (limited by the current spark gap and capacitor
configuration)  and  structural  enclosure  of  the  lower  floor.  Both  improvements  have  been  planned  and
The  most  valuable  academic  outcome  was  the  hands-on  engagement  with  resonance,  impedance,  and
high-frequency  electrical  phenomena  —  topics  that  are  rarely  explored  at  this  depth  in  secondary
education  and  that  provide  a  genuine  head-start  for  university-level  study  in  electrical  engineering  or
Salvaged from a discarded microwave oven
